does: This paper introduces EvoRubrics, a reinforcement learning framework that dynamically generates evaluation rubrics during policy training through adversarial co-evolution between a Policy LLM and a Rubric Generator. Rather than using fixed or periodically updated rubrics, the two components interact within each training step: the policy improves under guidance from current rubric criteria, while the rubric generator simultaneously adapts its evaluation standards to remain discriminative as the policy advances. The framework operates without requiring external frontier models or ground-truth answers, instead maintaining rubric relevance through continuous adversarial feedback. The authors demonstrate that this approach prevents reward saturation and exploitation that plague static rubrics, while naturally inducing a curriculum effect that guides policy improvement.
connects: This work extends recent research on rubric-based reward design for open-ended language model tasks, responding directly to limitations in prior dynamic rubric approaches that depend on external models or coarse-grained updates. It builds on the broader literature using interpretable criteria-based rewards as an alternative to learned reward models in domains where ground truth verification is infeasible. The co-evolutionary framework draws from multi-agent RL and adversarial training traditions, applying these principles to the problem of maintaining evaluation signal quality as policies improve. The work also connects to curriculum learning research by showing how adversarial rubric adaptation naturally produces progressive task difficulty scaling.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for more adaptive training paradigms in LLM reinforcement learning. The approach suggests pathways toward fully self-improving systems that don't rely on external scaffolding or human-provided evaluation benchmarks, potentially enabling RL at scale for tasks where comprehensive rubrics are difficult to specify a priori. The automatic curriculum effect could inform more general methods for tracking evolving model capabilities and adjusting evaluation difficulty accordingly. Future work might extend this to multi-policy scenarios, investigate theoretical properties of co-evolutionary dynamics in this setting, or combine EvoRubrics with other interpretability approaches to provide deeper insights into what distinguishes high-performing model behaviors during training.
